ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dried parsley as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for culinary herbs is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of dried parsley is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 1.5 g), used as a garnish or flavour enhancer in soups, salads, pasta, and meat dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Spices, parsley, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 02029)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried parsley (Petroselinum crispum) used as primary nutrient reference. Input HPB values are consistent with USDA data and are retained where they align; USDA values used to populate fields not present in HPB input (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, minerals). HPB Singapore data corroborates core macros.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Herbs and Spices — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database; dried herbs with moderate carbohydrate content estimated at GI ~52 (low category) consistent with published values for dried leafy herbs
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried parsley exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dried parsley contains 50.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g but is consumed in very small culinary doses (1–3 g per serving). GI is estimated at approximately 52 (low category) based on the general pattern for dried leafy herbs with high fibre content (26.7 g/100 g), which substantially attenuates glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 1.5 g serving = (52 × 50.64 / 100) × 0.015 ≈ 0.4 g, confirming negligible glycaemic impact at culinary doses.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (2683 mg/100 g) with negligible sodium contribution at culinary doses (~7 mg per 1.5 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Dried parsley is safe and beneficial for hypertensive individuals at culinary doses (1–3 g per serving), as its exceptionally high potassium content supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation, while the absolute sodium contribution per serving is negligible despite the elevated per-100 g figure of 452 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycaemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.4) and bioactive flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin) with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried parsley poses no meaningful glycaemic risk at typical culinary doses of 1–3 g per serving; its flavonoid content (apigenin and luteolin) has demonstrated insulin-sensitising and hypoglycaemic effects in preclinical studies, making it a suitable flavour enhancer for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.91 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high potassium, and polyphenol-rich anti-inflammatory profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried parsley is cardioprotective at culinary doses due to its very low saturated fat content, absence of trans fat, high potassium supporting vascular health, and rich polyphenol content (apigenin, luteolin, myristicin) that reduces oxidative stress and vascular inflammation; it is appropriate for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin) and very low saturated fat; high vitamin K content requires consistent intake for patients on warfarin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried parsley is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory polyphenol profile; patients on warfarin therapy should maintain consistent rather than variable intake to avoid interference with anticoagulation management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried parsley (Petroselinum crispum) is a dehydrated culinary herb produced by low-temperature drying of fresh flat-leaf or curly parsley leaves, resulting in concentrated green flakes with a mild, herbaceous, slightly grassy flavour and a dry, crisp texture. It is one of the most widely used dried herbs globally, appearing in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, European, and Western cuisines as a garnish, flavour base, and functional ingredient in soups, stews, pasta sauces, marinades, salad dressings, and spice blends. Nutritionally, dried parsley is exceptionally nutrient-dense per 100 g: it provides 292 kcal, 26.6 g protein, 5.5 g fat (of which only 0.9 g is saturated), and 50.6 g carbohydrate with 26.7 g dietary fibre. It is one of the richest plant sources of potassium (2683 mg/100 g), calcium (1140 mg/100 g), and iron (22 mg/100 g). It also contains high levels of vitamin C (125 mg/100 g), vitamin A (969 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin E (8.96 mg/100 g), and folate (180 mcg DFE/100 g). Critically, dried parsley is consumed in very small culinary doses of 1–3 g per serving, meaning its per-100 g nutrient density translates to trace absolute contributions per meal. Its bioactive compounds — apigenin, luteolin, myristicin, and chlorophyll — confer anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and mild hypoglycaemic properties. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' from the input record has been removed as it is not applicable to a dried herb.
common_user_queries: Is dried parsley good for high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in dried parsley per serving?

Can I use dried parsley instead of fresh parsley?
What vitamins are in dried parsley?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried parsley (NDB 02029). Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, vitamins, minerals) sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. Both are approved primary sources. The food is a well-characterised commodity ingredient with consistent published values across approved databases.
notes: 1. The input dietary_tags included 'wholegrain' which is not applicable to a dried herb and has been removed. 'high_protein' and 'high_fibre' are retained as accurate per-100 g descriptors, with the caveat that at culinary serving sizes (1–3 g) the absolute contribution is small. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dried parsley). vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for dried parsley). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.91 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.12 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02029). 4. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 52, low category) as no direct GI measurement for dried parsley exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; glycaemic load per 1.5 g serving is approximately 0.4 g, confirming negligible glycaemic impact at culinary doses. 5. SNOMED CT code not identified for dried parsley as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for culinary herbs is limited. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LT are used as the best available defensible ontology anchors for dried parsley; these should be verified against the latest FoodOn and FoodEx2 releases. 7. No multilingual local-language names are required for this food as it is a generic global ingredient not classified under any cuisine requiring mandatory local-language coverage per G6 rules.
